figures.caption: Table 2: Summary of evaluation benchmarks and metrics. We list evaluation metrics, average clip/video length (#Len), and the number of test samples (#Num).
Figure 17: Prompts for simplifying the steps generated by our pipeline to align with YouCook2 annotations [84] with DeepSeekR1. The full prompts can be found in our code.

Figure 8: Illustration of DownstreamTasks: Dense Video Captioning and Procedural Step Grounding. (a) Dense Video Captioning: the model generates timestamped, natural-language descriptions for every event in an instructional video. (b) Procedural Step Grounding: the model localizes each procedural step within its corresponding video segment. When fine-tuned on the DenseStep2M training set, individual steps may be out of order, redundant, or missing; when evaluated on the DenseCaption100 test set, all steps are human-annotated and perfectly ordered.

Figure 7: Illustrative R1-Score computation for instructional step alignment. This example shows how we (1) match each human-annotated action with one or more LLM-generated steps whose combined descriptions fully cover its key operations and objects—allowing synonymous phrasing (e.g., “spritz oil”↔ “add oil”), abstract-to-concrete conversions (e.g., “prepare a camera” ↔ “place the camera on the table”), step splitting or merging, and the omission of optional modifiers—and (2) merge timestamps for any Ground Truth step matched to multiple LLM steps by setting the start time to the earliest generated start and the end time to the latest generated end. From these aligned pairs, we then derive the R1 metrics (including Precision, Recall, F1-score and mIoU).
Figure 13: Prompts for matching textual and visual information with Qwen2.5. Relevant examples have been omitted for clarity, and the full prompts can be found in our code.
Figure 14: Prompts for generating instructional steps with Qwen2.5-VL. Relevant examples have been omitted for clarity, and the full prompts can be found in our code.

Figure 2: Three-step pipeline for Stage1 (Video Filtering and Segmentation). WhisperX [9] first generates noisy ASR transcripts with timestamps, followed by Qwen2.5 [76] for titling, segmentation, and coarse instructional filtering. DeepSeek-R1 [31] then refines the ASR text, and Qwen2.5-VL [8] finally verifies semantic alignment between video segments and the refined text. Videos with ≥75% aligned instructional content are retained.
Figure 10: Prompts for splitting the video and generating the title with Qwen2.5. Relevant examples have been omitted for clarity, and the full prompts can be found in our code.
Figure 15: Prompts for refining and merging steps with DeepSeek-R1. Relevant examples have been omitted for clarity, and the full prompts can be found in our code.

Table 6: Cross-Domain Video Retrieval Results across Ego-centric, Exo-centric, and Ego-Exo benchmarks. DS2M refers to the DenseStep2M dataset. Parentheses indicate absolute gains/losses over the respective baseline.

Figure 3: Examples of issues in initial step generation by Qwen2.5-VL [8]—such as disordered sequences, redundant or hallucinated steps, and inaccurate timestamps—addressed during Stage3 (Step Refining and Merging) by DeepSeek-R1 [31].
